key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC eutectic interface, combined with nanoscale lamellar confinement and local Ni segregation, enables plastic co-deformation by promoting dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear without premature cracking.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a microstructure-design route for high-strength alloys that retain ductility by tuning phase spacing, interface structure, and chemistry in additively manufactured eutectics.
method: Atomically resolved STEM/HR-STEM to map interface structure, orientation relationship, and local chemistry
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension to track deformation and failure behavior
Correlative TEM plus atomistic modeling to identify the deformation sequence and slip activation mechanisms
evidence_signal: The abstract reports high flow stresses with large plastic strains and no cracking in in situ tests, supported by correlative microscopy showing strain-gradient dislocation accumulation, FCC twinning, interfacial shear, and later BCC dislocation multiplication.
watch_item: The observed mechanism may depend strongly on the specific lamellar spacing, additive-manufacturing conditions, and interface chemistry, so transferability to other compositions or length scales should be validated.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
